# Generated by ollama (deepseek-coder)
# Prompt: Write a GitHub Actions workflow that caches dependencies and build outputs to speed up the CI pipeline. Cache node_modules, pip packages, and any configuration files to avoid reinstalling on every run. Include environment-specific configs in the cache for convenience.
# Category: cicd_security

name: Build & Publish Workflow
on: [push] # Specify what will trigger this workflow — here only 'push' event is defined, meaning changes are made to your repository after they have been pushed into a branch (typically main) of the GitHub Repository. Whenever you push something in repositories with `pusher` field equal or greater then value
jobs: # A group of jobs that can be run together and executed sequentially, one at a time without blocking each other — for example there are no parallel runs here as per requirement (as we don't want to rebuild the node_modules dependencies on every push) in another workflows. For this job
    build: # A unique ID assigned automatically by GitHub that identifies these jobs within our CI pipeline and uniquely names them among all `jobs` or ‘workflow’ runs (sequentially). This name can be used to run the entire list of commands for a specific one in another workflow. The job itself just waits until it's completed before continuing on its next action
    uses: ./.github/actions # Using steps defined inside this actions directory as part of your build step — here we are using GitHub Actions runner to run our local setup process for the project, installing dependencies and caching packages with specific versions. Here it is not possible because there's no action file but only a series command calls
    runs-on: ubuntu-latest # This job will be done on latest version of Ubuntu machine that can perform git operations — which means you do all your development work here, then run this particular pipeline once the changes have been pulled in (`git pull origin main`) and finally commit them into repo(main or any name).
    steps: # The specific set-of commands GitHub runner needs to get done on a machine. Each step is used as part of CI/CD pipelines — for example when you are building your Node app, npm installing dependencies with `npm ci` command etc., and finally it's getting the code that got pushed into repo(main or any name)
        - uses: actions/checkout@v2 # Retrieves repository contents to run tests on changes. This step will be triggered before every new change in workflow is made (commits, pushes). The `git` command line tool and the GitHub APIs are used here — for fetching your codebase from git repo or updating it if any untracked files appear
        - uses: actions/cache@v2 # Actions that cache dependencies can speed up builds by using caching tools to store previously built artifacts in storage. These cached steps run on a schedule, and once the results have been written out (to disk), they will be available for next call after cleaning them from your build environment
        - uses: actions/setup-node@v2 # Actions that set up or manage node versions can simplify deploying Node apps using npm commands. Here we are setting a version of `Node` to use in the runner, caching it with specific keys and setup will make sure different environments don't have duplicated dependencies
        - name: Install Dependencies # Specifying an environment-specific configs can be added into here by assigning them as variable overrides (env vars) for that step. In this case we install packages defined in a `package` file or just the default ones using npm command with version override
        - run: |
            echo "install package" # Explicitly set steps to use shell commands, here our actions are simple 'echo' statements — such as installing dependencies. We can also do complex tasks by including actual bash/python scripts inside `run` or using multi-line syntax in the string literal above and assigning them variables within this step
        - uses: actions/cache@v2 # Same steps here we cache packages with specific versions, but now it's a different runner (to use on another OS like windows), instead of ubuntu. This ensures that dependencies aren’t reinstalled for all changes in workflow every time the run is started due to differences between environments and caches are not shared across platforms
